original_abstract: While improved activity was recently reported for bimetallic iron-metal-nitrogen-carbon (FeMNC) catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acid medium, the nature of active sites and interactions between the two metals are poorly understood. Here, FeSnNC and FeCoNC catalysts were structurally and catalytically compared to their parent FeNC and SnNC catalysts. While CO cryo-chemisorption revealed a twice lower site density of M-N<sub>x</sub> sites for FeSnNC and FeCoNC relative to FeNC and SnNC, the mass activity of both bimetallic catalysts is 50–100% higher than that of FeNC due to a larger turnover frequency in the bimetallic catalysts. Electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy identified the coexistence of Fe-N<sub>x</sub> and Sn-N<sub>x</sub> or Co-N<sub>x</sub> sites, while no evidence was found for binuclear Fe-M-N<sub>x</sub> sites. <sup>57</sup>Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy revealed that the bimetallic catalysts feature a higher D<sub>1</sub>/D<sub>2</sub> ratio of the spectral signatures assigned to two distinct Fe-N<sub>x</sub> sites, relative to the FeNC parent catalyst. Furthermore, the addition of the secondary metal favored the formation of D<sub>1</sub> sites, associated with the higher turnover frequency.
humanized_abstract: Although recent studies show an improvement in catalytic performance of iron-based bimetal iron-metal-nitrogen-carbon (FeMNC) catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic condition, there is a lack of fundamental understanding of the roles of the second metal element and the interactions between the two metal elements. We investigate the structure and activity of FeSnNC and FeCoNC by comparing them with their parent iron-based catalysts, i.e., FeNC and SnNC. While the CO cryo-chemisorption shows that the site density of M-N<sub>x</sub> is two times lower in FeSnNC and FeCoNC than those in FeNC and SnNC, the mass activity of the bimetallic catalysts is 50–100% higher than that of FeNC with larger turnover frequency in bimetallic catalysts. The coexistence of Fe-N<sub>x</sub> and Sn-N<sub>x</sub> and Co-N<sub>x</sub> sites in the bimetallic catalysts is confirmed using electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) techniques. However, no signal of the binuclear Fe-M-N<sub>x</sub> species is observed. Additionally, <sup>57</sup>Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy indicates that the bimetallic catalysts exhibit higher D<sub>1</sub>/D<sub>2</sub> ratio of the spectral peaks corresponding to the Fe-N<sub>x</sub> species than that of the parent FeNC. The second metal element favors more the D<sub>1</sub> site corresponding to higher turnover frequency.

We investigate the structure and activity of FeSnNC and FeCoNC by comparing them with their parent iron-based catalysts, i.e., FeNC and SnNC. The coexistence of Fe-N<sub>x</sub> and Sn-N<sub>x</sub> and Co-N<sub>x</sub> sites in the bimetallic catalysts is confirmed using electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) techniques. Additionally, <sup>57</sup>Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy indicates that the bimetallic catalysts exhibit higher D<sub>1</sub>/D<sub>2</sub> ratio of the spectral peaks corresponding to the Fe-N<sub>x</sub> species than that of the parent FeNC. The second metal element favors more the D<sub>1</sub> site corresponding to higher turnover frequency.
